In this lesson, we're going to be talking about constructing your customer captivation offer. As I mentioned earlier, this is all about delivering value while marketing and then selling. As you heard me mention earlier, it's first and foremost about delivering value. Keep in mind that an individual is coming to your lead magnet offer page, they're entering their contact details for information, and then they're coming to the customer captivation offer page. We need to deliver value by fulfilling on the hook and promise that we made on the lead magnet offer page. I'm going to show you how to do that later on in module four. I also mentioned that the right way to do two step marketing is to offer a lead magnet, but that lead magnet also does the marketing and selling for you. The process is that we market, we're doing marketing, and then after we do our marketing, then we go into selling. This is something that most marketers don't understand. They don't understand the difference between marketing and selling. Let's talk about that. Good good. Marketing is all about your prospect. Marketing is talking about their prospect, talking about their problems, their needs, their desires, their wants. Selling is all about your product, it's all about the features of your product, the benefits of your product, the offer. See, any time you're talking about you, your company, your product, your offer, a reason of your product, that's selling. Marketing is when you're talking about the prospect and you're talking about the prospects needs and desires and wants. This is critical because as Peter Drucker said, the job of marketing is to make selling superfluous. The job of marketing is to make it so that you don't need to sell. You basically get to offer your product or service. You get to offer your solution and you've built enough desire, enough interest during the marketing. Marketing is everything that you say before you introduce your product or service. It's everything that you say, everything that you do, everything that you present to the prospect so that when it comes time to present your product or service or introduce them to your product or service, they're already sold on what it does and why they want it. That's the difference between marketing and selling. The better your marketing is, the easier the selling process becomes. Again, marketing is everything that you say and everything that you do before you introduce the prospect to your product or service. Our marketing is good enough, the offer becomes very easy to present to the prospect. Let's talk about the customer captivation presentation outline. You're going to be presenting your customer captivation offer via a video sales letter, via a VSL. The order of which you're going to present that information, that you're going to present the VSL is as follows. I'm not going to dive extremely deep into each one of these because we could literally put together a multi-hour course on VSL construction in and of itself. There are a lot of great courses out there that teach how to construct a VSL like John Benson's course, for example. I want to go through each of these elements because I want you to understand the purpose and what's going on here. Again, keep in mind that on the customer captivation offer page, and we're going to cover the elements of that page, there is a video sales letter. That video sales letter delivers the value as well as does the marketing and then does the selling by introducing your product or service. The VSL begins with your hook and promise. The hook and promise, keeping it congruent from the lead magnet offer page, keeping it congruent. This is the thing that is of most interest to your prospects. This is the thing that got them to enter their email address to request the free information. We start off by presenting your hook and then restating your promise. After you do that, you want to very quickly present your credentials. In other words, why should they listen to you as it relates to this topic, as it relates to this hook and promise? After you present your credentials, and those credentials could be experienced, those could be true credentials, they could be the fact that you were where the prospect is right now and now you're where they want to be. The point is just to credentialize why they should listen to you. The next step is to give them a reason why. In this case, it's why is this information important? In other words, why is it that they should pay extremely close attention to what it is that you have to say in the rest of this video? This is where you really restate the problem. In this portion of the VSL, you are restating the main problem that your prospects are having, the urgent problem that your solution is going to solve for them. You are stating the problem and then really what we could say is you are agitating the problem. You're stating the problem, you're reminding them of the problem, you're showing them the connection of what they're about to hear in this video to the problem that they're having and then you're agitating the problem. Going with the example that we've been using up to this point, the whole 54 years old and older and behind in your retirement savings. What we would want to do over here is say that, look, if you are 54 years or older and you are behind in your retirement, this is critical information because and then we agitate the problem. In other words, we really show them what is going to happen to them in the future if they don't take action. What is it that most people, most prospects in their situation that don't take action, that aren't watching this video, for example, what is the outcome going to be, the negative outcome for them? That's this section. Reason why. Problem and then we agitate the problem. When you state a problem, you need to offer proof and that's proof that this problem is legitimate. It's one thing to present or agitate a problem for a prospect, especially when it comes to the agitation phase where you're showing them the negative outcome of not taking action, the negative outcome of their current situation. When you say that, you cannot just automatically expect that prospects are going to believe you, that they're going to just accept what it is that you're saying as fact. You need to present proof that this is the case. You need to demonstrate one or more proof elements. I'm going to tell you that just as a quick aside, that when it comes to marketing, proof is one of the most critical aspects of your message because without proof backing up what it is that you're saying, all it is that you are doing is conveying claims. All it is that you're doing is making claims. This is how you turn a claim into an accepted fact. This is how you get prospects to believe and accept what it is that you are saying as true. It comes from the proof. You're going to see later on that we have another additional proof points that we utilize at a different strategic point in the VSL. We need to offer proof over here. We've got this reason why the problem, proof point, and agitate. These are all one in the same section right here. We're telling them what the problem is that they have. We're agitating the problem and we're providing proof. Then what we're doing in the next section is we're introducing them to the mechanism. This is now we're introducing them to the fact, going back to our example, that there is a new investing vehicle that will now allow them to double their net worth in five years or whatever that example was that I gave you earlier. We're now introducing them to this mechanism. We're almost saying, look, this is what you're dealing with right now. Here's the proof for it. What's going to happen to you if you don't take action today? Fortunately, there is, boom, a mechanism that benefits, that gives them benefits. Then we show them the benefits and we list out the benefits. We give them the benefits of this mechanism. Here is where at this point in the VSL, we're now showing them this is connected to the promise. We said earlier, right, there's this new investing vehicle that will allow you to double your net worth in five years. What you're going to find in the rest of this video is a simple system that you can use right now to get started with this new investing vehicle so that in the next 60 months, you see your net worth double or more. Why should you listen to me when it comes to this? Boom. You go into the right, the reason why, the problem, the proof, agitate or aggravate the problem, introduce your mechanism, and then you show the benefits of this mechanism. It's the benefits of this mechanism. In this case, the mechanism is the new investing vehicle going with this, continuing with our example. Then, of course, like I said, you need to provide proof. You can't just expect them to accept these benefits as fact. Remember, without proof, your statement of benefits simply become advertising claims and that's it. You need to have proof to back up what it is that you are saying. After you provide proof that this mechanism really delivers benefits, that's when you introduce your solution. This solution is your product or service. It's your solution that packages and delivers to them a usable approach, a usable process, a usable system for benefiting from this unique mechanism. You introduce your solution and then you give features, advantages, and benefits. On an upcoming slide, I'm going to break down for you the difference between advantages and benefits. Basically, you're giving them the features of your solution. You're giving them the advantages of each feature. You're giving them the benefits. Then what we're doing is after that, you are revealing the price. Here is where you reveal the price and you reveal the add to cart button. We're going to talk about the page development later on. For right now, I want you to understand you reveal the price and you reveal the add to cart button. That's when you have your first call to action. Call to action is really nothing more than to grab XYZ product. Go ahead and click the orange add to cart button below. After that first call to action, that's when you want to introduce your premium or bonus, your premiums or your bonus, your high value add on bonuses. You say, in addition to getting XYZ product, when you order today, you're also going to get and then you present the bonuses, of course, the features of the bonus as well as the benefits. Then you give your risk reversal statement. This is where you present your money back guarantee. In addition, as well, when you order today, you order completely risk-free because you get my XYZ guarantee. You're going to cover guarantee language and whatnot later on. Then you give a second call to action. To grab XYZ product, the free bonuses, all backed by the money back guarantee, go ahead and click the add to cart button below. The next point that you cover is an urgency reminder. This is if there's a price increase, if there's a limited quantity, you want to remind them of that. I'll tell you that in most cases, the better urgency reminder is to remind them of their problem and to remind them of what's going to happen if they don't take action. That's why what we do is the next point is we future pay. You remind them of their problem. You remind them of what's going to happen, like the likelihood, like, look, the reality is that people who don't take action are not going to be able to retire or they're not going to have enough money or they're going to have to live an impoverished lifestyle or whatever it is. You remind them of that. Then you future pace. Then you say that, but when you take action today and get XYZ, now you show them, you bring them into the future and you show them what their life can be like or will be like with your solution. You remind them of the problem of not taking action and the outcome of that. Then you future pace them with what their life can look like when they do take action. Then you give your final call to action. Go ahead, click the add to cart button below and grab your package. This is the outline of the customer captivation offer presentation. The thing that I want you to understand, we talked about the difference between marketing and selling. What I want you to see is that these sections right here are all marketing. The hook, the promise, the reason why, the problem, the proof points about the problem, aggravating the problem, this new mechanism that's out there that's available to them, the benefits of that mechanism to them, the proof that this is going to work for them. This is all marketing. This is all creating the desire for your mechanism, creating the desire to utilize your mechanism to escape from their problem. The rest is selling. The key is that we're not just jumping right to introducing your solution, introducing them to your product or service. We're starting with marketing. It's showing them the proof that the problem exists and helping them understand what their future is going to be like if they don't take action and do something about this problem and introducing them to this mechanism and showing them the benefits and providing the proof for these benefits. This whole section right here is what provides the value and fulfills on the hook and the promise that was made on the lead magnet offer page because you are in essence educating them. In the marketing funnel automation partnership coaching program, I teach a whole system called education-based marketing, EBM content, how we're actually using content, the appearance of content, educational, valuable content, but it's that content that happens to further the sales, that content that leads prospects closer to wanting to buy your product or service. That's what we're doing here on this page. In this whole sequence, the hook and the promise is what gets their attention. The presentation of your credentials tells them why they should listen to you. The reason why tells them why they need to pay attention to this information. The providing of proof demonstrates to them that what you're saying is true. The aggravation of the problem teaches them what's going to happen if they don't take action. The introducing of the mechanism is teaching them about an opportunity that they have now to relieve that problem. Showing them the benefits is teaching them about what they can experience with this mechanism. Then providing proof for them is teaching them that they should pay close attention to this, that this is true and that this is good. Then it's here where you're introducing them to your solution. This is where we begin the sales process. This is now basically you've taken this mechanism and you've put it into a package that you're now going to offer them. When you offer this package to them, now they are thankful when you've done this portion of the VSL correctly. I talked about feature advantage and benefit. Let's talk about what these are. A feature advantage benefit statement explains the feature, explains what the feature does and how it benefits the prospect. The FAB statement explains what the feature is, which is the features are facts, characteristics, aspects of your product or service. You give and explain an element of your product, then you give an advantage. Advantages are again what the features do and then you give the benefit of that advantage. Benefits answer why a prospect should value the advantage. In other words, it connects the facts about your product to a solution for your client, for your prospect. Let me give you an example of an FAB statement. This is the template, if you will. Because it has a feature, you will be able to do advantage and what that means for you is benefit. Here's this template taken into an example. Our sleeping bags have a one-inch layer of insulation on them. This helps to retain body heat on cold nights. You'll be warm all night, which means that you'll get a great sleep and be well rested for a day of fun activities that you'll enjoy fully. What you can see here is that this first statement is the feature. Our sleeping bags have a one-inch layer of insulation on them. What does the feature do? This is the advantage. It helps to retain body heat on cold nights so that you'll be warm. That is the advantage. What is the benefit? Which means that you'll get a great night's sleep and will be well rested for a day of fun activities that you'll enjoy. That is an example of an FAB statement. Let me give you some questions to help flesh out features, advantages, and benefits. First is why might the feature that you are talking about be advantageous to the prospect? What does it mean for the prospect? What does it offer to the prospect? Why might the prospect like it? These are four questions that you can ask yourself when you're looking at the different features of your product or service to help flesh out the FAB statements.
